attack_chain.description: Attacker harvested long-term AWS access keys from a publicly exposed Postman server where credentials were stored in the project's variables.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we came across a publicly exposed Postman server with access key credentials stored in the project's variables

attack_chain.description: Attacker authenticated to AWS using stolen long-term IAM access keys from a non-customary hosting-provider IP with a Kali Linux aws-cli user agent.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys.

evidence_chunks.quote: The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys.
The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys.

attack_chain.description: Attacker issued IAM ListUsers API calls (denied) to enumerate accounts in the environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Here we saw three API calls: (two) list users, and one GetSendQuota ... All were denied

attack_chain.description: Attacker ran GetCallerIdentity (whoami-equivalent) with multiple access keys from the same source IP to determine the identity/privileges of each stolen key.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker used the API GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys and from the same IP

evidence_chunks.quote: the attacker used the API GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys and from the same IP
the attacker used the API GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys and from the same IP

attack_chain.description: Attacker called SES GetSendQuota to retrieve sending limits for the Amazon SES account, reconnaissance for email-abuse capability.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: GetSendQuota was an Amazon email service feature that "provides the sending limits for the Amazon SES account."

attack_chain.description: Attacker called UpdateAccountSendingEnabled multiple times to toggle SES email sending on/off across the AWS account/region — preparing SES for abuse (e.g., spam/phishing).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: "UpdateAccountSendingEnabled," specifically, seemed interesting ... enables or disables email sending across your entire Amazon SES account

attack_chain.description: Attacker leveraged seven additional compromised IAM accounts/access keys in parallel, repeating the same reconnaissance and SES-related activity.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker was attempting to use seven different access keys and accounts

evidence_chunks.quote: the attacker was attempting to use seven different access keys and accounts
the attacker was attempting to use seven different access keys and accounts

techniques.evidence_quote: The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys.
a publicly exposed Postman server with access key credentials stored in the project's variables
three API calls: (two) list users ... GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys
GetSendQuota ... provides the sending limits for the Amazon SES account
UpdateAccountSendingEnabled ... enables or disables email sending across your entire Amazon SES account
This attacker's focus on email infrastructure was noteworthy

initial_access_vector: Stolen long-term AWS IAM access keys harvested from a publicly exposed Postman server where the keys were stored as project variables.
persistence_mechanism: Continued use of valid long-term IAM access keys (and 7 additional compromised keys) for access; toggling of SES account-level sending via UpdateAccountSendingEnabled.
privilege_escalation: None observed; attacker relied on permissions already granted to the stolen IAM keys (many enumeration calls were denied).
